startScreenOptions.title: AN25.1-AN25.9 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for AN25.1-AN25.9.

interactions.label: True ribs (ribs 1–7) are distinguished from false ribs (8–10) and floating ribs

params.question: True ribs (ribs 1–7) are distinguished from false ribs (8–10) and floating ribs (11–12) by which characteristic?

answers.text: True ribs are longer than false and floating ribs

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Rib length varies individually and is not the distinguishing feature. Classification is based on the type of anterior attachment to the sternum.

answers.text: True ribs have costal cartilage that attaches directly to the sternum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Ribs 1–7 are called vertebrosternal ribs because their costal cartilage connects directly to the sternum, unlike false ribs (which connect indirectly via the cartilage above) and floating ribs (which have no sternal connection).

answers.text: True ribs lack a costal groove, unlike false ribs

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: All typical ribs (3rd–9th) have a costal groove along the inferior border of the shaft housing the intercostal neurovascular bundle, regardless of whether they are true or false ribs.

answers.text: True ribs articulate with only one vertebral body

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Most typical ribs articulate with two adjacent vertebral bodies via costal demifacets on the vertebral body. The number of vertebral articulations does not distinguish true from false ribs.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the three rib groups: true ribs (1–7) attach directly to the sternum, false ribs (8–10) attach indirectly via the cartilage of the rib above, and floating ribs (11–12) have no sternal attachment at all.
Correct! True ribs connect directly to the sternum via their own costal cartilage — this direct sternal attachment is the key distinction from false and floating ribs.

interactions.label: A patient with a subphrenic abscess complains of right shoulder tip pain. Which

params.question: A patient with a subphrenic abscess complains of right shoulder tip pain. Which anatomical relationship best explains this referred pain?

answers.text: The intercostal nerves supplying the diaphragm also innervate the shoulder

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The diaphragm is not supplied by intercostal nerves for motor function. Its sole motor nerve is the phrenic nerve, arising from cervical roots C3, C4, and C5.

answers.text: The phrenic nerve arises from C3, C4, C5, and the C4 dermatome includes the shoulder tip

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The phrenic nerve (C3–C5) is the sole motor supply to the diaphragm. Since C4 also supplies the shoulder tip dermatome, diaphragmatic irritation is perceived as shoulder pain — a classic example of referred pain.

answers.text: The vagus nerve carries pain fibres from the diaphragm to the shoulder

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The vagus nerve provides sensory innervation to viscera such as the oesophagus, but it does not carry somatic pain from the diaphragm to the shoulder. The phrenic nerve is responsible.

answers.text: The subphrenic abscess directly compresses the brachial plexus roots

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A subphrenic abscess sits beneath the diaphragm in the upper abdomen, far from the brachial plexus in the neck and axilla. The shoulder pain is referred, not caused by direct compression.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the phrenic nerve: it arises from C3, C4, C5 and is the sole motor supply of the diaphragm. Since the C4 dermatome includes the shoulder tip, diaphragmatic irritation refers pain to the shoulder.
Correct! The shared C4 dermatome between the diaphragm (via the phrenic nerve) and the shoulder tip is a classic anatomical explanation for referred pain.

interactions.label: A trauma patient arrives with a tension pneumothorax requiring emergency needle

params.question: A trauma patient arrives with a tension pneumothorax requiring emergency needle decompression. Which surface landmark guides correct needle placement?

answers.text: Fifth intercostal space at the midclavicular line

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is the location of the cardiac apex beat, not the site for needle decompression. Inserting a needle here risks cardiac injury.

answers.text: Second intercostal space at the midclavicular line

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The second intercostal space at the midclavicular line is the standard site for emergency needle decompression in tension pneumothorax, providing safe access to the pleural cavity.

answers.text: Fourth intercostal space at the midaxillary line

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The midaxillary line at the fourth or fifth intercostal space is used for chest drain (tube thoracostomy) insertion, not for emergency needle decompression.

answers.text: Sixth intercostal space at the anterior axillary line

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is not a standard site for needle decompression. The second intercostal space at the midclavicular line is preferred for its safe, predictable access to the pleural space.

overallFeedback.feedback: For tension pneumothorax, needle decompression is performed at the second intercostal space at the midclavicular line. The fifth intercostal space at the midclavicular line is where the apex beat is palpated — confusing these two landmarks could be dangerous.
Correct! The second intercostal space at the midclavicular line is the landmark for needle decompression — do not confuse it with the apex beat location at the fifth intercostal space.
